flashcards.front: What is the best-case time complexity of Bubble Sort?
flashcards.back: O(n) when array is already sorted due to optimized implementations with flag checks.

flashcards.front: Which sorting algorithm uses divide-and-conquer strategy?
flashcards.back: Merge Sort and Quick Sort both use divide-and-conquer approach.

flashcards.front: What is the main disadvantage of Selection Sort?
flashcards.back: O(n²) time complexity in all cases, even when partially sorted.

flashcards.front: Why is Merge Sort considered stable?
flashcards.back: Preserves relative order of equal elements during merging phase.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of Heap Sort?
flashcards.back: O(1) as it sorts in-place using heap structure.

flashcards.front: When is Insertion Sort most efficient?
flashcards.back: Small datasets or nearly sorted data due to adaptive nature.
Partitioning can swap equal elements from opposite ends.

flashcards.front: Which algorithm has worst-case O(n log n) time?
flashcards.back: Merge Sort maintains O(n log n) in all cases.

flashcards.front: What is the pivot selection impact in Quick Sort?
flashcards.back: Poor pivot choice leads to O(n²) worst-case performance.

flashcards.front: Why is Counting Sort not comparison-based?
flashcards.back: Uses key values as indexes in auxiliary array.

flashcards.front: What's the main advantage of Timsort?
flashcards.back: Combines Merge Sort and Insertion Sort for real-world data efficiency.

flashcards.front: Which sort is preferred for linked lists?
flashcards.back: Merge Sort due to sequential access pattern.
